content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
February 20, 1997
Honorable William L. Todd, Jr.
Wmgert, Grebing, Anello & Brubaker, LLP
600 West Broadway, Seventh Floor
San Diego, California 92101-3370
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-039
Dear Justice Todd:
This is in response to your request for advice concerning the conflict of interest disclosure
provisions ofthe Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
QUESTION
You are a retired Associate Justice ofthe Fourth District Court of Appeal. During 1996,
you tried one superior court case of fewer than 30 days. You have asked iftime spent acting as a
discovery referee and conducting settlement conferences should be counted for purposes of
determining whether you are required to file a statement of economic interests (Form 700).
DISCUSSION
In order to avoid conflicts of interest, the Act requires public officials, including judges
and court commissioners, to ifie periodic statements disclosing their personal assets and income
and to disqualify themselves from making or participating in governmental decisions which may
affect their personal financial interests. (Sections 8 1002(c), 87100
Commission regulation 18724, as amended, clarifies the filing requirements for part-time
or temporary court commissioners, pro tern judges, and retired judges:
“(a) Temporary or part-time court commissioners and pro
‘Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2,
sections 18100-18995 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


William L. Todd, Jr.
Our File No. A-97-039
Page 2
tern or temporary judges shall file statements of economic interests
pursuant to Government Code Sections 87200 et seq., if they serve
or expect to serve 30 days or more in any calendar year. Provided,
however, that this section shall not excuse these individuals from
any filing obligations imposed by a conflict ofinterest code adopted
pursuant to Government Code Sections 87300 et seq.
(b) Retired judges who serve on assignment pursuant to
Article VI, Section 6 of the California Constitution shall ifie annual
statements of economic interests pursuant to Government Code
Sections 87200 et seq., for each calendar year in which they serve
30 days or more. Statements filed by retired judges shall cover the
period January 1 through December 31 ofthe calendar year in
which 30 days or more were served, and shall be filed no later than
March 1 ofthe following year. Such statements shall be ified with
the Fair Political Practices Commission.”
This regulation governs your service as ajudicial officer, whether sitting on assignment as
a judge pro tern or as a court commissioner, for purposes of determining ifyou have served or
may serve 30 days or more in a calendar year.
2 On the other hand, employment by private parties
as a discovery referee, arbitrator, mediator or the like, does notcount toward the 30-day
regulatory threshold. Therefore, ifyou are employed by private parties to assist in resolving a
dispute, whether or not that dispute is in litigation, this employment is not counted toward the 30-
day reporting threshold. If you are employed by the court, however, that service is counted under
regulation 18724.
Please contact me at (916) 322-5660 ifyou have additional questions.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Carla Wardlow
Chief, Technical Assistance Division
2
Enclosed is a copy of the Ahern Advice Letter, No. A-93-030, in which it was determined
that court referees are generally not considered judges or court commissioners under the Act
unless a particular referee has been appointed as a court commissioner under a statute authorizing
that appointment.
